item.title: Meridian 100th Anniversary | Preserving Taveuni, one photo at a time
item.description: FOR decades, thousands of photographs sat quietly inside drawers, briefcases and old boxes in a family home on Taveuni, waiting for someone to discover the stories they held. Today, those images are breathing life into the island’s past, prompting calls for Meridian Store managing director Champak Lal Dayaram to establish a small museum or an art gallery so future generations can experience Taveun
item.content:encoded:
FOR decades, thousands of photographs sat quietly inside drawers, briefcases and old boxes in a family home on Taveuni, waiting for someone to discover the stories they held.

Today, those images are breathing life into the island’s past, prompting calls for Meridian Store managing director Champak Lal Dayaram to establish a small museum or an art gallery so future generations can experience Taveuni’s rich history through the lens of one man.

The photographs were taken by his late father, Ambaram Dayaram, whose passion for photography documented everyday life on the Garden Island long before smartphones and digital cameras existed.

His collection captures a changing Taveuni, its people, villages, schools, chiefs, sporting events, businesses and celebrations, creating what many now regard as one of the island’s most valuable historical records.

During an interview, Mr Dayaram was asked whether there were any plans to do so, he said he have received a lot of requests to do so.

“I have a large collection. They include community events, chiefly installations, sporting events and many other moments from Taveuni’s history,” he said.

Photography, he said, was never his father’s profession.

Instead, it was a lifelong hobby driven by genuine passion.

“Photography was his hobby, but he was very passionate about it. He was the first person here to take passport photographs.

“He photographed people from all walks of life for passport applications, driver’s licences and government documents.

“Back then everything was done in black and white, and passport photographs did not require a plain white background.”

His camera became a familiar sight across the island.

Teachers, police officers, traditional leaders, business owners and ordinary villagers all found themselves standing before his lens.

“He also photographed schoolteachers, police officers, chiefs and ordinary members of the community.

“Those photographs have become an important historical record.”

Among the treasured images is one that holds special significance.

“The earliest photograph I have is of our first shop. There was nothing around it at the time.

The collection, however, almost remained hidden forever.

He said it was only while renovating a section of a store that he stumbled upon boxes containing decades-old photographic negatives.

“I found boxes of my grandfather’s negatives when we were renovating and moving things around.

“They were scattered in drawers and briefcases. I realised they needed to be preserved.”

Recognising their historical value, he purchased specialised equipment to convert the fragile negatives into digital images before time could destroy them.

“I bought a scanner that converts negatives into digital images.

“Every time I scan one, another piece of Taveuni’s history comes back to life.”

The restoration project has produced emotional moments for many island families.

“Many families have seen photographs of their grandparents for the very first time because of this collection.”

The archive continues to grow with every scanned negative.

Together, the photographs tell the story of an island evolving through the decades.

“These photographs document schools, villages, community events, chiefs, festivals, early businesses, airports and many other parts of Taveuni’s history.

“They are more than family photographs; they are part of the island’s history.”

item.title: Meridian 100th Anniversary | No plans to reopen the landmark 180th Meridian Cinema
item.description: THERE are no plans to reopen the landmark 180th Meridian Cinema in Wairiki, Taveuni . The once vibrant cinema now stands silent in Wairiki, its weathered walls serving as a reminder of an era when movies brought communities together.
item.content:encoded:
THERE are no plans to reopen the landmark 180th Meridian Cinema in Wairiki, Taveuni .

The once vibrant cinema now stands silent in Wairiki, its weathered walls serving as a reminder of an era when movies brought communities together.

It’s managing director Champak Lal Dayaram says there are no plans to reopen it as a cinema.

He said the challenges facing modern cinemas were far greater than simply restoring the building.

“It depends on market demand. Today there are many challenges,” he said.

“Businesses across Fiji are facing labour shortages, and operating a cinema requires skilled people.”

Mr Dayaram said cinemas had changed dramatically over the decades.

Unlike the old days when films arrived on reels, today’s cinemas rely on digital technology.

“Modern cinemas are digital, so operators need computer skills to download and manage films. The building also requires maintenance.” The cinema currently stands in ruins, opposite the busy Meridian shopping complex in Wairiki.

Once filled with laughter, applause and excitement, it now sits unattended after years of deterioration.

One of the major setbacks came during a lengthy wait for the renewal of the land lease.

“Another issue was the land lease. It took about 18 years to renew, and during that time the building deteriorated.

“Some of the structural columns have weakened, and repairs would be costly.”

Although restoration is technically possible, he believes rebuilding the structure is only part of the challenge.

“The biggest challenge is not rebuilding the cinema, it is ensuring it can operate sustainably.”

He said people’s viewing habits had changed significantly.

Streaming services now dominate the entertainment industry. “People now have YouTube, Netflix, Prime Video and other streaming services.

“Before investing in reopening, we have to know there is enough demand.”

Mr Dayaram said previous studies estimated restoring the cinema would require around half a million dollars.

However, investing such a large amount without confidence in long-term viability would be difficult.

Instead of reopening as a traditional cinema, the family once explored another option.

“We once planned to convert it into an events centre. It could host meetings, training sessions, church camps and community functions.

“We even developed plans for that idea.”

While the proposal showed promise, it has yet to move beyond the planning stage.

Built in 1954 by the late Ambaram Dayaram, it became one of Fiji’s largest cinemas during its prime.

It was also famous for being the only cinema in the world where patrons could cross into a new day simply by changing seats because of its location along the 180th Meridian. The building hosted movies, talent contests, public debates and many community activities.

It became a meeting place for residents from both the northern and southern ends of Taveuni.

The first movie screened was His Majesty O’Keefe, while Bollywood productions later became favourites among local audiences.

Today the empty structure continues to attract interest from visitors and former patrons who remember the important role it played in island life.

For many, it remains a symbol of Taveuni’s cultural history.

item.title: Meridian 100th Anniversary | End of an era…Dayaram Fakir passed away in 1952
item.description: WHEN Dayaram Fakir passed away in 1952, the future of the Meridian Store depended on the strength and determination of his family. The responsibility of keeping the business running fell on his wife, Narbada Ben Fakir, who became known as the iron lady for the way she carried the family through one of its most difficult periods.
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Dayaram Fakir passed away in 1952, the future of the Meridian Store depended on the strength and determination of his family.

The responsibility of keeping the business running fell on his wife, Narbada Ben Fakir, who became known as the iron lady for the way she carried the family through one of its most difficult periods.

For Narbada Ben, it was not only about running a shop.

She had to look after her children, manage the household and ensure that the business her husband built continued to serve the people of Wairiki, Taveuni.

The master of ceremony of the store’s 100th anniversary Larry Thomas said Ms Ben played an important role after her husband’s passing.

“After Dayaram’s death, the burden of running the household and business and caring for nine children fell on Narbada Ben,” he said.

Dayaram Fakir established the business in 1926 after arriving in Taveuni from Gujarat, India.

The small shop, which started as Dayaram Fakir Tailors, Drapers and Grocers, later became the Meridian Store.

Over the years, Dayaram and Narbada Ben built a family of nine children.

Their eldest daughter was born in India, while the other children were born in Fiji.

The family’s life was centred around the business.

The store provided for the family, but it also became a place where the children learned the importance of hard work and responsibility.

However, the family faced a major setback when Dayaram passed away in 1952.

Suddenly, Narbada Ben had to carry the responsibility of the business and the future of her children.

“She managed the home, the store, the finances and the community needs.

“It was her resilience that kept the business going through decades of hardship.”

At a time when business operations were not easy, Narbada Ben worked tirelessly to ensure customers continued receiving the goods they depended on.

The Meridian Store supplied essential items to the community, including groceries, clothing and other daily necessities.

Her strength helped protect the legacy that Dayaram had started.

As the children grew older, they also became part of the business.

The store became a family workplace where everyone played a role.

Following his father’s death, Ambaram Dayaram became the custodian of the Meridian Store.

He continued expanding the business by strengthening supply routes, trading copra, serving the Catholic missions in Wairiki and providing goods to families across Taveuni.

“He became a trusted figure in the community and continued the legacy of what his father began.”

The values passed down by Narbada Ben continued through the next generations.

The children of Ambaram and Narbada later moved to different parts of the world in search of opportunities.

Today, members of the family live in India, England, Canada, the United States, Australia and New Zealand.

He said returning to Wairiki was about remembering the sacrifices made by their parents and grandparents.

“Success is when you can return home from whichever part of the world you are to come celebrate the legacy of your grandfather and parents,” he said.

The Meridian Store centenary celebration became a reunion of the Fakir family, with members travelling from different countries to honour the journey of their ancestors.

item.title: TOURISM TALANOA | Missing framework | Why mangrove protection stalls
item.description: Now and then, an issue sits quietly in the background while the louder national conversations take over. In the past few weeks, understandably, much of Fiji’s public attention has been on the national budget, the cost of doing business, infrastructure needs, public spending, taxation, aviation, investment and growth. These are important issues, and tourism has a direct stake in all of them.
item.content:encoded:
Now and then, an issue sits quietly in the background while the louder national conversations take over.

In the past few weeks, understandably, much of Fiji’s public attention has been on the national budget, the cost of doing business, infrastructure needs, public spending, taxation, aviation, investment and growth. These are important issues, and tourism has a direct stake in all of them.

But as we look ahead to World Mangrove Day on July 26, I believe it is also time to bring mangroves back into the national conversation.

Not as a side issue. Not as an environmental talking point that only matters to technical officers, conservation groups or coastal communities. But as part of Fiji’s national resilience, tourism future and long-term development planning.

We are part of the Mangrove Restoration Working Group that brings together government agencies, technical partners, civil society organisations and other stakeholders working through the practical details of national mangrove restoration.

Development – most often tourism – has been the primary driver behind the removal of mangroves, cleared to make way for resorts and other commercial infrastructure. Yet despite the critical ecological role mangroves play in coastal protection, biodiversity, and climate resilience, not enough is being done to ensure they are replaced or restored. While today’s custodians of the environment may be making better efforts, historically there has been poor oversight and weak enforcement of regulations. This has allowed unchecked destruction to continue, leaving gaps in coastal defences and undermining Fiji’s long-term sustainability.

We have recognised that stronger policy reforms, backed by consistent monitoring and enforcement, are urgently needed to reverse this legacy of neglect and ensure that mangrove ecosystems are not sacrificed for short-term economic gain. And we have called for these reforms, committed to supporting efforts to bring them about and take part in the forums that address these. The role and key focus of the working group is on mangrove restoration. Mangrove protection is dealt with through the Mangrove Management Committee, although the two areas naturally overlap. Restoration cannot be separated from protection, just as coastal resilience cannot be separated from land-use planning, development approvals, community access, fisheries, tourism and climate adaptation.

That is where tourism has a very real interest.

Fiji’s tourism industry is built around our natural environment. That might sound obvious, but it is worth repeating because we sometimes discuss tourism only in terms of rooms, arrivals, air seats, taxes and investment values. Those are obviously important. But our real product is still the environment and its many facets that anchor our branding and marketing and ultimately are the foundation of the visitor experience. From coastlines to highland ridges, our reefs, beaches, forests, rivers, culture, communities and marine ecosystems are what makes Fiji so diverse and special.

Mangroves are part of that foundation. They protect shorelines, reduce wave energy, stabilise sediment, support fisheries, store carbon, provide habitat for marine and bird life, and help buffer coastal communities against flooding and storm surge. In tourism areas, they may sit beside villages, resorts, jetties, coastal roads, dive operations, kayaking routes, fishing grounds and community-based tourism sites.

Mangroves are not simply trees growing in mud. They are living infrastructure – natural seawalls, nurseries for marine life, and carbon sinks – that remain under-appreciated and consistently under-estimated in their true worth. Treating them as expendable vegetation ignores the fact that they are vital assets for coastal protection, biodiversity, and climate resilience.

With Fiji actively pushing for more tourism investment and Government clear about the need for more rooms, more investment, more connectivity and stronger economic returns from the sector, we must ensure we’re aware of the natural destruction these efforts might also bring about. We understand the ambition, but growth must be matched with concern for environmental protection and resilience.

If we are pushing for more tourism infrastructure in coastal areas, then the natural systems that protect those areas must also be given priority. We cannot speak seriously about increasing accommodation capacity, expanding tourism zones and strengthening investor confidence while leaving coastal resilience, mangrove restoration and regulatory clarity fading slowly into the background.

For tourism operators, the practical issues are familiar. Coastal erosion affects beaches and access. Poor drainage affects properties, roads and nearby communities. Storm surges – a climate phenomenon that has increased in frequency over the last decade – affect safety and operations. Damaged reefs and mangroves affect fisheries, marine biodiversity and the visitor experience. Unclear rules affect whether an operator can maintain existing access, restore a degraded area, support community planting, repair drainage, build a boardwalk, or respond after a weather event.

This is where clarity on regulations matters.

One of the persistent frustrations raised around the national mangrove programme is that the regulations governing mangrove protection have still not been finalised or passed. Partners across agencies have indicated that this has been pending for several years. This delay is disappointing because the message this sends is that this is unimportant in the grand scheme of things, while the absence of a clear and finalised regulatory framework makes everyone’s work harder. Without it, enforcement remains inconsistent, restoration efforts lack certainty, and stakeholders are left navigating a patchwork of unclear obligations. Finalising these regulations is therefore not just a bureaucratic step; it is essential to give coherence, authority, and momentum to the collective work of safeguarding mangroves.

Even something as basic as a working definition of mangroves matters. It determines how areas are mapped, how restoration sites are selected, what species are prioritised, what activities are allowed, what requires approval, and how different agencies interpret their roles.

The working group is trying to progress important technical work, including restoration priorities, baseline mapping, species guidance, site-selection criteria, data standards and wider national coordination. But that work needs a firm policy and regulatory foundation. Without it, agencies, communities, NGOs and the private sector are all trying to move in the right direction while still waiting for the rules of the roadmap to be properly marked.

Completing this should not be a complicated request. We would like to see the relevant mangrove regulations progressed with urgency, and we would like the final framework to be clear, practical and usable because this will obviously provide the guidelines for how the industry approaches their individual efforts in this area.

It should clarify what is protected, which agency leads on which matter, when the Environment Management Act and EIA process apply, how forestry-related provisions interact with development activity, and what operators, communities and landowners are required to do before undertaking works near mangroves. As opposed to the current working environment, where everyone does what they think is right and then deals with the consequences of an agency disagreeing, development or protection programs getting halted, and the usual exchange of follow-ups continues for months if not years.

It should also distinguish between very different types of activity. Clearing mangroves for reclamation is not the same as maintaining an existing drainage outlet. Building new infrastructure is not the same as restoring a degraded mangrove edge. Emergency and clearance work after flooding are not the same as planned development. Community access, traditional use, tourism access, navigation and conservation management all need to be understood properly and guided clearly. Without that clarity, well-intentioned people hesitate, responsible operators wait, communities become uncertain, and poor decisions can still slip through because the system is not as clear as it should be.

The tourism industry has a critical role to play in this conversation.

Operators often see coastal changes early. They know where erosion is worsening, where drainage is failing, where sediment build-up is causing challenges for accessibility, where guests can no longer safely access a beach, where jetties are exposed, where mangroves are recovering, where they are being damaged, and where community use and tourism activity overlap. That site-level knowledge can help inform national mapping and restoration priorities.

Tourism also has a strong interest in nature-based solutions. The draft national approach to coastal protection recognises that seawalls should not be treated as the only answer. In some places, hard engineering may be necessary. In others, nature-based or hybrid approaches, including mangrove restoration, reef protection, dune restoration, vetiver planting and better land-use planning, may deliver stronger long-term outcomes.

That distinction matters. A wall that protects one small strip of land but worsens erosion next door, damages beach amenity, traps floodwater or cuts off access is not a holistic solution. It may simply move the problem further down the road. Good coastal protection must look at the whole system.

For Fiji, that system includes communities, customary rights, public infrastructure, private investment, cultural values, marine ecosystems and the visitor economy.

The working group’s work may sound technical from the outside, with discussions around definitions, mapping, species, restoration criteria, baselines and management responsibilities. But these are the building blocks of better national decision-making, and why we’re so engaged.

World Mangrove Day is celebrated on July 26, and Fiji has an opportunity to make mangroves more visible again with practical national attention, with clearer regulations, stronger coordination and restoration priorities that recognise ecological, community and economic value. We need tourism included early where coastal protection, access, drainage and infrastructure decisions affect tourism areas. We need to treat mangroves as part of the country’s resilience infrastructure.

And we need to do this now, while Fiji is still shaping the next phase of tourism growth.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Meridian 100th Anniversary | The night the cinema became a boxing ring
item.description: THE story of Taveuni’s famous Dateline 180th Meridian Cinema did not begin with a movie. Instead, the first crowd to pack into the landmark building gathered to watch punches being thrown inside a boxing ring.
item.content:encoded:
THE story of Taveuni’s famous Dateline 180th Meridian Cinema did not begin with a movie.

Instead, the first crowd to pack into the landmark building gathered to watch punches being thrown inside a boxing ring.

Before the cinema became known for screening Bollywood blockbusters, British documentaries and Hollywood classics, it opened its doors with a boxing tournament after unexpected delays prevented the first scheduled movie screening.

Meridian Store managing director Champak Lal Dayaram said many people assumed the first event at the cinema was a film.

However, history tells a different story.

“When we opened our cinema, we introduced 35-millimetre films, which provided a much better viewing experience,” he said.

“The Tat family actually operated the first cinema on the island, showing 16-millimetre films such as newsreels, cartoons and British documentaries.

“We wanted to provide something bigger and better for the people of Taveuni.”

The first feature film eventually screened at the cinema was His Majesty O’Keefe, starring Burt Lancaster, a fitting choice because parts of the movie had been filmed in Fiji.

But before audiences could settle into their seats for that historic screening, construction delays forced the family to change their plans.

“Interestingly, our first event was not a movie screening.”

“We faced delays in opening the cinema, so our first event became a boxing tournament.

“I even have photographs of that event, which was held inside the cinema.”

The boxing contest attracted large crowds eager to see what everyone had been talking about.

The building itself had already become the talk of the island.

Constructed in 1954 by the late Ambaram Dayaram after securing a loan from the then Bank of New Zealand, the cinema was built during a time when transporting building materials to Taveuni was a major challenge.

Every piece of timber, steel and equipment had to make the difficult journey across the sea before finally reaching Wairiki.

Despite the obstacles, the family succeeded in creating what became one of Fiji’s largest cinemas during its heyday.

The cinema later earned worldwide recognition as the only theatre where patrons could cross into a new day simply by changing seats because it was located along the 180th Meridian.

After the boxing tournament, the cinema quickly established itself as Taveuni’s entertainment hub.

People travelled from both the northern and southern parts of the island to watch movies, attend talent quests, public debates and community events.

Movie nights became major social occasions.

Crowds regularly filled every available seat during screenings held on Thursdays, Saturdays and Sundays.

Mr Dayaram said operating a cinema in those days demanded patience and discipline.

“The cost of running movies on a rental basis costs a fortune.

“We had cinemas like Damodar Brothers and Para Brothers supplying us with films.

“In anything involving 300 people it could get rowdy and out of control at any time, so operating a cinema required control and patience.”

Unlike today, cinemagoing was more than entertainment.

It was a community gathering where families, friends and neighbours met, shared stories and spent time together.

The introduction of 35-millimetre projection also transformed the viewing experience for island audiences.

item.title: Meridian 100th Anniversary | A workplace built on family
item.description: FOR more than a decade, Pooja Kumar has been part of the Meridian Store journey, witnessing the growth of a business that has become more than just a place of work. As one of the longest-serving staff members at the Wairiki, Taveuni business, Ms Kumar said her years with the company have been filled with learning, support and strong relationships.
item.content:encoded:
FOR more than a decade, Pooja Kumar has been part of the Meridian Store journey, witnessing the growth of a business that has become more than just a place of work.

As one of the longest-serving staff members at the Wairiki, Taveuni business, Ms Kumar said her years with the company have been filled with learning, support and strong relationships.

“It has been a very good experience working at Meridian Store,” she said.

Ms Kumar said the leadership and guidance from the management team had helped staff develop both professionally and personally.

“Our boss really looks after us. He teaches us many things, especially with the business, how to serve customers, how to manage stocks and how to understand the way the business operates.”

She described Meridian Store managing director Champak Lal Dayaram as someone who maintained high standards and encouraged staff to always strive for excellence.

“He is a perfectionist. He wants everything to be done properly.

“He wants the shop to be clean, organised and for staff to maintain a high standard.”

She said those lessons had helped staff members build confidence and improve their approach to work.

“For some of us, we are naturally shy, but working here has helped us become more confident.

“It is like an icebreaker where you come out of your comfort zone, interact with customers and be yourself.”

Beyond the workplace, she said the company had also provided important support to staff and their families, especially during challenging periods.

She recalled the coronavirus pandemic as a difficult time for many people, but said Meridian Store ensured its employees remained employed.

“During that difficult time, everyone kept their jobs. Nobody was laid off.”

She said this support meant a lot to staff members who had families and responsibilities.

“They do not see us only as workers. They treat us as part of their family.”

For Ms Kumar, the 100-year-old business represents more than trade and commerce.

It represents people, relationships and a workplace where staff feel valued and respected.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Meridian 100th Anniversary | Giving back to the community
item.description: GIVING back to a community that has supported their family over the years remains a strong commitment for Meridian Store managing director Champak Lal Dayaram. For Mr Dayaram, the contribution towards community projects, including their contribution to the construction of a school.
item.content:encoded:
GIVING back to a community that has supported their family over the years remains a strong commitment for Meridian Store managing director Champak Lal Dayaram.

For Mr Dayaram, the contribution towards community projects, including their contribution to the construction of a school.

He said his family had always believed in supporting the community and helping where they could, especially in areas that directly benefited children and education.

“We have committed ourselves to supporting community projects and contributing to the construction of a school,” he said.

He said the initiative was a way of giving back to the people who had been part of his family’s journey.

“Through this initiative, we hope to create better opportunities for children and strengthen the community.”

He said his family understood the important role education played in shaping the lives of young people and preparing them for the future.

“Our commitment reflects our belief in giving back and making a positive difference in the lives of others.”

He said education was one of the greatest gifts that could be provided to children because it opened doors to new opportunities.

“We understand that education plays an important role in shaping the future of young people.”

He said supporting the construction of a school was an investment in the next generation and a way to provide children with a safe and supportive place to learn.

“By helping to build a school, we are investing in the next generation and creating a safe place for learning.”

He said the project was made possible through the support and dedication of many people who shared the same vision.

“The project is a result of teamwork, dedication, and the support of many people.”

He thanked those who contributed their time, resources and efforts towards making the project a reality.

“We are grateful to everyone who has contributed their time, resources, and efforts towards making this vision a reality.”

He said the school would become a lasting contribution that would continue to benefit students, teachers and the wider community for many years.

“The school will serve as a foundation for knowledge, growth, and development.”

For the Dayaram family, giving back is a way of honouring the community that has walked alongside them and ensuring future generations have the opportunity to succeed.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Meridian 100th Anniversary | Dayaram Fakir a tailor by trade
item.description: LONG before clothing stores lined Fiji’s towns and ready-made garments became easily available, people on Taveuni relied on the skilled hands of a tailor to stitch everything from coats and shirts to ceremonial attire. That tailor was Dayaram Fakir, whose craftsmanship would eventually lay the foundation for what is now Meridian Store.
item.content:encoded:
LONG before clothing stores lined Fiji’s towns and ready-made garments became easily available, people on Taveuni relied on the skilled hands of a tailor to stitch everything from coats and shirts to ceremonial attire.

That tailor was Dayaram Fakir, whose craftsmanship would eventually lay the foundation for what is now Meridian Store.

According to Meridian Store managing director Champak Lal Dayaram, his grandfather arrived on Taveuni as a professional tailor at a time when the island was still developing and access to clothing was limited.

“My grandfather, Dayaram Fakir, was a tailor by trade,” Mr Dayaram said.

When Dayaram Fakir settled on Taveuni, Wairiki was the centre of activity on the island because of the Catholic Mission.

The mission attracted people from different parts of Fiji and overseas, making the area a busy hub for worship, trade and administration.

“At the time, Wairiki was the centre because of the Catholic Mission,” Mr Dayaram said.

“My grandfather was here because he also provided services to the missionaries.”

His tailoring skills quickly became sought after by a wide range of customers.

“There was a demand for clothing from the chiefs, colonial officials, missionaries and the Girmit descendants who were already living here.”

Mr Dayaram said travelling to Levuka or Suva to have clothes made was neither practical nor affordable for many island residents.

In those days, a trip by boat could take several days, depending on the weather and sea conditions.

“There was a real need for tailoring services,” he said.

Instead of making the long journey, people turned to the local tailor, who could produce clothing suited to both everyday life and important occasions.

As Christianity spread throughout Taveuni, clothing became an increasingly important part of community life.

Church services, weddings and traditional gatherings encouraged people to dress more formally than they had in previous generations.

“The missionaries encouraged people to dress well for church, and that created more demand,” Mr Dayaram said.

He believes his grandfather’s work went beyond simply sewing garments.

By introducing new tailoring techniques and clothing styles, he helped influence the way many people on the island dressed.

“Perhaps that was also when the sulu vakataga with pockets became more common as new styles were introduced.”

While there is no written record identifying exactly who first designed the pocketed sulu vakataga, Mr Dayaram believes local tailoring helped popularise practical improvements that reflected the changing needs of customers.

Demand for clothing continued to grow as more people settled on the island.

Chiefs required formal attire for traditional ceremonies.

Missionaries needed suitable clothing for church work.

Colonial officials expected well-made garments for official duties.

Families also wanted clothing for weddings, funerals and other important events.

Every garment was measured, cut and sewn by hand.

There were no factories producing ready-made clothing, making the skills of a tailor both valuable and respected.

As the business expanded, customers began asking for more than tailoring services.

“They wanted fabric to make clothes and purchased sulus for special occasions.”

item.title: CLEAR VISION FOR A BRIGHTER FUTURE | How frequently do we need an eye check-up?
item.description: CLEAR VISION FOR A BRIGHTER FUTURE | How frequently do we need an eye check-up?
